concepts.name: Kossel-Lewis Approach to Chemical Bonding
concepts.explanation: Atoms combine to achieve stable noble gas configurations (octet or duplet) by either transferring electrons (ionic bond) or sharing electrons (covalent bond). Lewis represented valence electrons as dots around the symbol.
concepts.formula_reaction: Na → Na⁺ + e⁻; Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻; Na⁺ + Cl⁻ → NaCl

concepts.name: Octet Rule
concepts.explanation: Atoms tend to combine in such a way that each atom acquires eight electrons (octet) in its valence shell, similar to the nearest noble gas configuration (ns²np⁶), except hydrogen which attains a duplet (1s²).

concepts.name: Lewis Symbols
concepts.explanation: Lewis symbols represent the valence electrons of an atom as dots placed around the chemical symbol. The number of dots corresponds to the group number for representative elements.

concepts.name: Ionic Bond (Electrovalent Bond)
concepts.explanation: An ionic bond is formed by the complete transfer of one or more electrons from a metal (low ionization enthalpy) to a non-metal (high negative electron gain enthalpy), resulting in electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions.

concepts.name: Covalent Bond
concepts.explanation: A covalent bond is formed by the mutual sharing of one or more pairs of electrons between two atoms, each atom contributing one electron to the shared pair, to attain stable octet configurations.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., H· + ·H → H:H; Cl· + ·Cl → Cl:Cl

concepts.name: Lewis Structures of Molecules
concepts.explanation: Lewis structures represent the arrangement of atoms, bonding pairs (shared electrons), and lone pairs (unshared electrons) in a molecule. The least electronegative atom is usually the central atom, and all atoms should achieve octet (except H).

concepts.name: Formal Charge
concepts.explanation: Formal charge is the difference between the number of valence electrons in a free atom and the number of electrons assigned to it in a Lewis structure (lone pair electrons + half of shared electrons). It helps select the most stable resonance structure.
concepts.formula_reaction: Formal charge = V - (L + ½S) where V = valence electrons, L = lone pair electrons, S = shared electrons.

concepts.name: Limitations of Octet Rule – Incomplete Octet
concepts.explanation: In some compounds like BeH₂, BCl₃, and AlCl₃, the central atom has less than eight electrons in its valence shell (incomplete octet). This occurs with elements having less than four valence electrons.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., BeH₂ (Be has 4 electrons), BCl₃ (B has 6 electrons).

concepts.name: Limitations of Octet Rule – Odd-electron Molecules
concepts.explanation: Molecules with an odd number of electrons, such as NO and NO₂, cannot satisfy the octet rule for all atoms because one atom will always have an unpaired electron.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., NO (11 valence electrons), NO₂ (17 valence electrons).

concepts.name: Limitations of Octet Rule – Expanded Octet
concepts.explanation: Elements in the third period and beyond (e.g., P, S, Cl) have d-orbitals available and can accommodate more than eight electrons in their valence shell, forming compounds like PF₅, SF₆, and H₂SO₄.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., PF₅ (P has 10 electrons), SF₆ (S has 12 electrons).

concepts.name: Lattice Enthalpy
concepts.explanation: Lattice enthalpy is the energy required to completely separate one mole of a solid ionic compound into its constituent gaseous ions. It stabilizes ionic compounds, compensating for endothermic ionization and electron gain steps.
concepts.formula_reaction: NaCl(s) → Na⁺(g) + Cl⁻(g); ΔlatH = +788 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Bond Length
concepts.explanation: Bond length is the equilibrium distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms in a molecule. It is measured spectroscopically and is the sum of the covalent radii of the two atoms.

concepts.name: Bond Angle
concepts.explanation: Bond angle is the angle between the orbitals containing bonding electron pairs around the central atom in a molecule. It determines the geometry and is measured spectroscopically.

concepts.name: Bond Enthalpy
concepts.explanation: Bond enthalpy is the amount of energy required to break one mole of a particular type of bond in gaseous molecules. For polyatomic molecules, average bond enthalpy is used (e.g., O–H bond in water is 464.5 kJ mol⁻¹).
concepts.formula_reaction: H₂(g) → H(g) + H(g); ΔH = 435.8 kJ mol⁻¹.

concepts.name: Bond Order (Lewis Concept)
concepts.explanation: Bond order is the number of bonds between two atoms in a molecule. It is 1 for single bonds, 2 for double bonds, and 3 for triple bonds. Higher bond order correlates with shorter bond length and higher bond enthalpy.

concepts.name: Resonance
concepts.explanation: Resonance occurs when a single Lewis structure cannot accurately represent a molecule. The actual structure is a hybrid (resonance hybrid) of two or more canonical structures (e.g., O₃, CO₃²⁻, CO₂). Resonance stabilizes the molecule.
concepts.formula_reaction: Represented by a double-headed arrow (↔) between canonical forms.

concepts.name: Polarity of Bonds and Dipole Moment
concepts.explanation: A polar covalent bond has unequal sharing of electrons due to electronegativity difference, creating a dipole moment (μ = Q × r). Dipole moment is a vector quantity; the net dipole moment of a molecule depends on the vector sum of individual bond dipoles.
concepts.formula_reaction: μ = Q × r; 1 Debye (D) = 3.33564 × 10⁻³⁰ C m.

concepts.name: VSEPR Theory – Postulates and Repulsion Order
concepts.explanation: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) theory states that electron pairs (bonded and lone) around a central atom repel each other and arrange themselves to minimize repulsion. The repulsion order is: lp-lp > lp-bp > bp-bp.

concepts.name: VSEPR – Shapes with No Lone Pair on Central Atom
concepts.explanation: When the central atom has no lone pairs, the geometry is determined solely by the number of bond pairs: AB₂ = linear (180°), AB₃ = trigonal planar (120°), AB₄ = tetrahedral (109.5°), AB₅ = trigonal bipyramidal (90°/120°), AB₆ = octahedral (90°).
concepts.formula_reaction: Examples: BeCl₂ (linear), BF₃ (trigonal planar), CH₄ (tetrahedral), PCl₅ (trigonal bipyramidal), SF₆ (octahedral).

concepts.name: VSEPR – Shapes with Lone Pairs on Central Atom
concepts.explanation: Lone pairs occupy more space and cause distortion: AB₂E (bent, e.g., SO₂), AB₃E (trigonal pyramidal, e.g., NH₃, bond angle 107°), AB₂E₂ (bent, e.g., H₂O, bond angle 104.5°), AB₄E (see-saw), AB₃E₂ (T-shape), AB₅E (square pyramidal), AB₄E₂ (square planar).

concepts.name: Valence Bond Theory (VB Theory)
concepts.explanation: Valence Bond Theory explains covalent bond formation as the overlap of half-filled atomic orbitals. As two atoms approach, new attractive forces outweigh repulsive forces, lowering potential energy to a minimum at the equilibrium bond distance.

concepts.name: Orbital Overlap Concept
concepts.explanation: Covalent bonds result from the partial interpenetration (overlap) of valence atomic orbitals of the two atoms. The greater the overlap, the stronger the bond. Overlap can be positive (bonding), negative (antibonding), or zero.

concepts.name: Sigma (σ) Bond
concepts.explanation: A sigma bond is formed by the head-on (end-to-end or axial) overlap of atomic orbitals along the internuclear axis. It is a strong bond, and a single bond is always a sigma bond.

concepts.name: Pi (π) Bond
concepts.explanation: A pi bond is formed by the sidewise overlap of parallel atomic orbitals (usually p-orbitals) perpendicular to the internuclear axis. It is weaker than a sigma bond and exists only in multiple bonds (double or triple).

concepts.name: Hybridisation – Introduction and Salient Features
concepts.explanation: Hybridisation is the intermixing of atomic orbitals of slightly different energies to form new equivalent hybrid orbitals with the same shape and energy. The number of hybrid orbitals equals the number of combining atomic orbitals.

concepts.name: sp Hybridisation
concepts.explanation: One s and one p orbital mix to form two equivalent sp hybrid orbitals arranged linearly (180°). It has 50% s-character and 50% p-character. Examples: BeCl₂ (linear) and C₂H₂ (ethyne, with two π bonds).
concepts.formula_reaction: Be: 1s²2s² → excited 1s²2s¹2p¹ → two sp hybrids.

concepts.name: sp² Hybridisation
concepts.explanation: One s and two p orbitals mix to form three equivalent sp² hybrid orbitals in a trigonal planar arrangement (120°). It has 33% s-character. Examples: BCl₃ (trigonal planar) and C₂H₄ (ethene, with one π bond).
concepts.formula_reaction: B: 1s²2s²2p¹ → excited 1s²2s¹2p² → three sp² hybrids.

concepts.name: sp³ Hybridisation
concepts.explanation: One s and three p orbitals mix to form four equivalent sp³ hybrid orbitals in a tetrahedral arrangement (109.5°). Examples: CH₄ (tetrahedral), NH₃ (pyramidal due to lone pair, 107°), H₂O (bent due to two lone pairs, 104.5°), and C₂H₆.
concepts.formula_reaction: C: 1s²2s²2p² → excited 1s²2s¹2p³ → four sp³ hybrids.

concepts.name: sp³d Hybridisation
concepts.explanation: One s, three p, and one d orbital mix to form five equivalent sp³d hybrid orbitals in a trigonal bipyramidal geometry. Example: PCl₅, where axial bonds are slightly longer and weaker than equatorial bonds.
concepts.formula_reaction: P: 3s²3p³ → excited 3s¹3p³3d¹ → five sp³d hybrids.

concepts.name: sp³d² Hybridisation
concepts.explanation: One s, three p, and two d orbitals mix to form six equivalent sp³d² hybrid orbitals in an octahedral geometry (90°). Example: SF₆, where sulphur has 12 electrons in its valence shell.
concepts.formula_reaction: S: 3s²3p⁴ → excited 3s¹3p³3d² → six sp³d² hybrids.

concepts.name: Molecular Orbital Theory (MO Theory) – Introduction
concepts.explanation: MO theory describes bonding by combining atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals that are polycentric (belong to the whole molecule). Bonding MOs lower energy and stabilize; antibonding MOs raise energy and destabilize.
concepts.formula_reaction: Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO): ψ_MO = ψ_A ± ψ_B

concepts.name: Conditions for Combination of Atomic Orbitals in MO Theory
concepts.explanation: For atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals, they must have: (1) comparable energies, (2) the same symmetry about the molecular axis, and (3) maximum overlap.

concepts.name: Types of Molecular Orbitals (σ and π)
concepts.explanation: σ MOs are symmetrical about the internuclear axis (e.g., σ1s, σ*1s from s-s or s-pz overlap). π MOs are not symmetrical; they have electron density above and below the axis (e.g., π2px, π*2px from p-p sidewise overlap).

concepts.name: Energy Level Diagrams for MOs (Order of Filling)
concepts.explanation: For O₂ and F₂: σ1s < σ*1s < σ2s < σ*2s < σ2pz < (π2px = π2py) < (π*2px = π*2py) < σ*2pz. For B₂, C₂, N₂: σ1s < σ*1s < σ2s < σ*2s < (π2px = π2py) < σ2pz < (π*2px = π*2py) < σ*2pz.

concepts.name: Bond Order and Stability from MO Theory
concepts.explanation: Bond order (b.o.) = ½ (Nb - Na), where Nb is the number of electrons in bonding MOs and Na in antibonding MOs. A positive bond order indicates a stable molecule; zero or negative indicates instability.
concepts.formula_reaction: Bond order = (Nb - Na) / 2

concepts.name: Magnetic Nature from MO Theory
concepts.explanation: If all electrons in MOs are paired, the molecule is diamagnetic (repelled by magnetic field). If there are unpaired electrons (one or more singly occupied MOs), it is paramagnetic (attracted). e.g., O₂ is paramagnetic.

concepts.name: MO Configurations – H₂, He₂, Li₂
concepts.explanation: H₂: (σ1s)² (b.o.=1, stable). He₂: (σ1s)²(σ*1s)² (b.o.=0, unstable). Li₂: KK(σ2s)² (b.o.=1, diamagnetic, exists in vapour phase).

concepts.name: MO Configurations – C₂ and N₂
concepts.explanation: C₂: KK(σ2s)²(σ*2s)²(π2px² = π2py²) (b.o.=2, diamagnetic, double bond consists of two π bonds). N₂: KK(σ2s)²(σ*2s)²(π2px² = π2py²)(σ2pz²) (b.o.=3, triple bond, diamagnetic).

concepts.name: MO Configuration – O₂ (Paramagnetism)
concepts.explanation: O₂: KK(σ2s)²(σ*2s)²(σ2pz)²(π2px² = π2py²)(π*2px¹ = π*2py¹). b.o. = 2. Contains two unpaired electrons in π* orbitals, explaining its paramagnetic nature (a key success of MO theory).

concepts.name: Hydrogen Bonding – Definition and Cause
concepts.explanation: Hydrogen bond is a weak electrostatic attraction between a hydrogen atom bonded to a highly electronegative atom (F, O, or N) and another electronegative atom in a different molecule or the same molecule.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., H⁺⁺ – F⁻⁻ – – – H⁺⁺ – F⁻⁻ (represented by dotted line).

concepts.name: Types of Hydrogen Bonding – Intermolecular and Intramolecular
concepts.explanation: Intermolecular H-bonding occurs between different molecules (e.g., H₂O, HF, alcohols) and affects boiling points and solubility. Intramolecular H-bonding occurs within a single molecule (e.g., o-nitrophenol) and affects physical properties.
